summary: Reviewed the brand event-feed changes, focusing on API exposure, secret handling, SSRF controls, and data integrity around the new per-brand feed credentials. The main risk is that brand management endpoints shown in the pack have no server-side authentication while now accepting event feed secrets and configuration that drive server-side fetches. The implementation does include some positive validation and avoids returning raw API keys to the client, but the current surfaces still permit material data-integrity and abuse scenarios.
appears_sound: Raw event feed API keys are not mapped back into public Brand responses; rowToBrand only exposes event_feed_has_key in lib/brands/brandRepo.ts:24.
Event feed URLs are constrained to HTTPS and literal localhost/private IPs are rejected before create/update in app/api/brands/route.ts:38 and app/api/brands/[id]/route.ts:44.
BaronsHub fetches include a timeout, no-store cache, and bearer authorization in lib/eventFeed/baronshubAdapter.ts:162.

findings.description: The brand collection API is reachable without any server-side auth while POST can create brands containing event feed credentials; an unauthenticated attacker can create or list brand records and influence server-side event-feed behavior. This is OWASP A01 broken access control and becomes more serious now that this endpoint accepts secret-bearing configuration.
findings.evidence: GET and POST in app/api/brands/route.ts:17 and app/api/brands/route.ts:23 call listBrands/createBrand after parsing input, with no getUser/session/role check in the handler.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: The app may intentionally rely on network-level protection or middleware not present in the review pack.
findings.what_would_confirm: Verify whether these /api/brands routes are protected by middleware or deployment access controls; otherwise add an explicit authenticated admin check in the route.

findings.description: The per-brand API allows unauthenticated reads, updates, and deletes of brand records; an attacker who knows or discovers a brand id can alter event-feed URLs/API keys or delete production brand data. This is a direct data-integrity and privilege-escalation path, not just a UI bypass.
findings.evidence: GET, PUT, and DELETE in app/api/brands/[id]/route.ts:20, app/api/brands/[id]/route.ts:30, and app/api/brands/[id]/route.ts:76 call getBrand/updateBrand/deleteBrand without any auth or permission check.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: A route group or middleware outside the pack could enforce auth before these handlers run.
findings.what_would_confirm: Check middleware coverage for /api/brands/[id]; if absent, require server-side admin authorization before every read/write/delete.

findings.description: Changing the default brand is a two-step non-transactional update, so concurrent creates/updates can leave multiple defaults or no intended default. That can corrupt brand resolution because getDefaultBrand uses maybeSingle and then falls back only if no default exists.
findings.evidence: createBrand and updateBrand first unset other defaults at lib/brands/brandRepo.ts:153 and lib/brands/brandRepo.ts:173, then separately insert/update the target row at lib/brands/brandRepo.ts:158 and lib/brands/brandRepo.ts:178.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: If the database has a partial unique index or trigger enforcing one default brand, this race may be mitigated, but the migration in the pack does not show one.
findings.what_would_confirm: Inspect the existing brands table constraints or add a transactional RPC/unique partial index for is_default=true.

findings.description: The SSRF guard only checks literal hostnames and does not resolve DNS, so a configured HTTPS hostname that resolves to 127.0.0.1, RFC1918 space, or link-local metadata can pass validation and later be fetched server-side. Exploit scenario: attacker controls a DNS name, configures it as the feed base URL, and causes /api/generate to make internal network requests.
findings.evidence: validateEventFeedUrl in lib/brands/validation.ts:43 calls isPrivateIp(parsed.hostname), while isPrivateIp in lib/brands/validation.ts:13 only checks string prefixes and localhost literals.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: The deployment environment may block egress to private networks, or only trusted admins may configure feed URLs once auth is fixed.
findings.what_would_confirm: Resolve configured hostnames at write time or fetch time and reject private/link-local results, including IPv6 variants and DNS rebinding-sensitive hosts.

findings.description: PUT cannot clear a stored event_feed_api_key; sending an empty key omits the field and preserves the existing secret even when event_feed_type is changed to none. This creates stale secret retention and can unintentionally reactivate old credentials if the feed type is later changed back.
findings.evidence: app/api/brands/[id]/route.ts:61 only sets dbInput.event_feed_api_key when rawApiKey is non-empty, and the comment at app/api/brands/[id]/route.ts:59 explicitly says omission preserves the existing DB value.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: The product may intentionally require a separate key-rotation/delete control, but no such control is shown in the pack.
findings.what_would_confirm: Define the desired secret lifecycle; if disabling a feed should remove credentials, explicitly set event_feed_api_key to null when event_feed_type is none or provide a dedicated clear-key operation.

findings.description: API error responses echo raw database error messages, which can expose table names, constraint names, or validation internals to unauthenticated callers. An attacker can submit invalid feed configs to map schema details and operational constraints.
findings.evidence: app/api/brands/route.ts:82 and app/api/brands/[id]/route.ts:69 return err.message directly, while brandRepo throws messages containing Supabase errors such as lib/brands/brandRepo.ts:162.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: This may be acceptable for an internal-only admin tool, but the handlers themselves do not show access restrictions.
findings.what_would_confirm: Confirm route exposure; otherwise log detailed errors server-side and return generic client errors.

empty_categories: No raw event_feed_api_key is returned in Brand JSON responses in the changed mapping code.
No SQL string interpolation or shell command injection was introduced in the reviewed changes.
No client-side localStorage storage of the event feed API key was introduced; the form keeps it transient and sends it only on save.
